data.title: China stocks post biggest monthly gain in 11 months
data.description:
China stocks closed up on Friday, capping their strongest monthly gain since September 2024, as abundant liquidity continues to drive the rally despite warnings from tech firms following recent price surges.

Hong Kong shares ended the week slightly lower.

** China's blue-chip CSI300 Index closed 0.7% higher, while the Shanghai Composite Index was up 0.4%. Hong Kong benchmark Hang Seng rose 0.3%.

** For the month, the CSI300 Index was up 10%. The Hang Seng Index was down 1% this week and has risen 0.9% in August.

** China's recent market rally has been underpinned by abundant liquidity in a low-yield environment, alongside government efforts to curb aggressive price competition to boost inflation.

** The daily turnover in onshore Chinese shares hovered around 3 trillion yuan ($419.41 billion) this week, with the total turnover for August set to hit a record high.

** China's top economic planner will work with other departments to investigate and punish below-cost dumping, false propaganda and speed regulation of "disorderly competition" in some industries, its spokesperson, Li Chao, said on Friday.

** Chinese chip firm Cambricon Technologies on Thursday issued a risk alert to investors in a stock exchange filing, citing a sharp rise in its stock prices since late July. Its shares fell 6% on Friday, after they more than doubled this month.

** Trading in the shares of China's Dosilicon was suspended on Friday after the semiconductor maker flagged multiple instances of abnormal stock price volatility since July 29.

** The tech-heavy STAR50 index slid 1.7% after surging nearly 30% this month, while consumer staples gained 2%, leading onshore advances as investors rotated into defensives.

** Tech majors trading in Hong Kong rose 0.5%.

($1 = 7.1529 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: Number of unemployed people in Germany passes 3 million mark
data.description:
The number of unemployed people in Germany passed 3 million for the first time in a decade, labour office figures showed on Friday, as the labour market of Europe's biggest economy is squeezed by two years of economic contraction.

The mark was reached in seasonally unadjusted terms, with an increase of 46,000 in the number of people out of work.

However, when accounting for seasonal trends, the office said the number of unemployed decreased by 9,000 in seasonally adjusted terms to 2.96 million.

The seasonally adjusted jobless rate remained stable at 6.3%, in line with a forecast by analysts in a Reuters poll.

"The labour market is still marked by the economic downturn of recent years - however, there are also first signs of stabilisation," said labour office head Andrea Nahles.

The unexpected decline in the seasonally adjusted number comes as Germany battles persistent economic weakness. The tariffs announced by U.S. President Donald Trump could put the economy on track for a third year without growth for the first time in history.

Despite the monthly improvement, unemployment remains elevated compared with pre-pandemic levels.

The Ifo Employment Barometer fell slightly in August to 93.8 points, down from 94.0 points in July.

“The labour market is still stuck in crisis,” said Klaus Wohlrabe, head of surveys at Ifo. “The stagnating economy is making companies act cautiously when it comes to personnel planning.”

The German economy has contracted for two consecutive years. As labour market indicators tend to lag other economic indicators, the fall in gross domestic product in the second quarter also suggests weakness ahead.

data.title: Bulgaria Producer Prices Rise for 9th Month
data.description:
Producer prices in Bulgaria increased by 8.6% year-on-year in July 2025 from 7.3% advance in the previous month.

This marked the ninth consecutive month of producer inflation, as prices rose further in mining and quarrying (24.5% vs 16.4%) and electricity, gas, steam and air-conditioning supply (21.0% vs 16.3%).

On the other hand, costs eased for manufacturing (7.8% vs 8.7%).

On a monthly basis, producer prices rose by 3.4%, recovering from a 0.2% decline in the preceding period.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0807 GMT - SK Telecom could post a 3Q net loss due to a penalty for a cybersecurity breach, Korea Investment & Securities analyst J.C. Kim writes in a note. Earlier this week, the South Korean wireless carrier was fined KRW134.79 billion by Seoul's Personal Information Protection Commission for failing to protect customer data from a hacking incident detected in April. Kim expects the company's 3Q earnings to be pressured by additional one-off costs for handing out temporary discounts to service charges in August and enhancing cybersecurity measures. Still, he maintains a buy rating and a target price of KRW61,000 on the stock, noting the penalty was in line with market expectations. Shares closed 1.3% lower at KRW54,200. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0430 GMT - Meituan made the right decision to defend its position in the food-delivery sector, says Morningstar's Chelsey Tam in a note. The Chinese shopping-and-delivery platform is avoiding Alibaba's "strategic misstep" of not matching Pinduoduo's aggressive user subsidies during the latter's rise, she says. Meituan subsidizing costs preserves its network, while the platform's variety offsets its lower-than-peer subsidies per meal, she adds. Tam reckons the market is focusing on medium-term uncertainty in the food-delivery market, overlooking a possible long-term earnings recovery as competition stabilizes. The analyst reduces her 2025-2027 EBIT forecasts by an average of 67% to reflect Meituan's higher subsidies, while raising projections for 2028-2034. Morningstar trims its fair-value estimate to HK$151 from HK$164. Shares rise 0.2% to HK$101.90. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0404 GMT - Inari Amertron's recent joint acquisition of Lumileds with Sanan Optoelectronics will remain a key focus for investors in the near term, Affin Hwang IB analyst Kevin Low says in a note. Losses at Lumileds are a concern, but there is upside from potential intellectual-property gains and stronger module assembly capabilities, he writes. Lumileds's global reach could also support Inari's expansion, including its Yiwu facility in China, Low says. "We firmly believe that Inari can execute well and leverage its partner to enhance overall capabilities and drive growth." Affin Hwang raises the target price to MYR2.79 from MYR2.77 while maintaining a buy rating on the stock. Shares are 3.0% higher at MYR2.04. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0332 GMT - Meituan could double its investment in instant delivery to 20 billion yuan in 3Q, potentially causing a large loss, CGS International analysts say in a note. The Chinese shopping-and-delivery platform targets a 2% gross transaction value margin for the segment over the longer term amid its rivalry with Alibaba, they write. While Meituan's delivery business gained users in 2Q, its in-store division was weighed by the food-delivery price war, they add, which the company reckons could continue in 3Q. The analysts cut their 2025-2027 adjusted EPS forecasts by 32%-125% on lower margins. CGS International trims the target to HK$123.00 from HK$161.00 but reiterates its add rating, saying it thinks Meituan can defend its leadership in the on-demand delivery market. Shares rise 0.6% to HK$102.30. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0153 GMT - Axiata Group's 2H core EPS could be weaker than 1H's, as losses at its 36.9%-owned associate XLSmart could deepen, CIMB Securities analyst Foong Choong Chen says in a note. He expects Axiata's core profit to remain under pressure through 2025-2026 given XLSmart's integration cost, before rebounding in 2027 once XLSmart delivers merger synergies. Upside could come from better valuations on asset sales, while delays or low pricing from asset monetization pose key risks, he adds. CIMB downgrades Axiata's rating to hold from buy due to limited upside potential, but raises its target price to MYR2.60 from MYR2.55. Shares are unchanged at MYR2.58. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1700 GMT - Pure Storage's strong 2Q report reflects widespread growth across categories, but it's tough to find an exact catalyst for the results, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note. "Our only conundrum is we can't point to any exact factors that are catalyzing PSTG's outperformance now specifically," the analysts say, noting competitor NetApp's relatively disappointing results suggest any pickup in storage sales likely isn't broad based. Nevertheless, with better trends across the board, the analysts raise their price target on Pure Storage's stock to $80 from $70. Shares rise 31% to $79.62. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1635 GMT - Pure Storage continued to make progress on its engagement with Meta and other hyperscaler customers in its latest quarter, setting the stage for a key long-term growth driver, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. Pure Storage management noted Meta initiated its first volume deployments, resulting in the first revenue contributions from the collaboration, and the company is looking to enable its other hyperscaler customers to replace raw storage with its DirectFlash Modules. The analysts say revenue contributions from such engagements will be minimal this fiscal year, but will be a key component of expectations for further accelerating growth in the future. They raise their price target on the stock to $80 from $70. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1605 GMT - Pure Storage is now on track to accelerate growth this year and beyond after seeing significant revenue momentum in its latest quarter, helped by broad-based strength across its portfolio, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. The company's raised guidance reflects its growing confidence in customers' spending intentions as the underlying macroeconomic environment remains more resilient than feared earlier in the year, the analysts say. Pure Storage's revenue performance and boosted outlook stand out relative to peers with more modest upsides and consistent outlooks, they say, noting such competitors are more exposed to a pullback in public sector spending. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)Delivery Hero reported first-half results. "Delivery Hero's Adjusted Earnings Guidance Cut Likely to Disappoint Investors — Market Talk," at 1043 GMT, misstated the company name.

1128 GMT - Delivery Hero's results show solid growth and improving profitability, Mwb Research's Alexander Zienkowicz writes in a note. Regional trends have improved since the first quarter, with Asia rebounding as Foodpanda returned to growth and Woowa's in-house delivery expanded. Meanwhile, the Americas sustained double-digit top-line growth and Ebitda turned positive, he says. Operational momentum remains strong, cash flow and the company's balance sheet have strengthened, he adds. The only setback was foreign exchange rate headwinds, which led to a cut in full-year adjusted Ebitda guidance. Shares are down 1.9% at 23 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1042 GMT - Delivery Hero reported solid first-half results but the cut to 2025 adjusted Ebitda guidance is likely to weigh on shares, Citi analysts write in a note. The downgrade sets adjusted Ebitda at between 900 million euros and 940 million euros, down from the previously guided range of 975 million euros and 1.025 billion euros. This implies a 16% shortfall versus consensus, they say. Shares are down 1.9% at 22.99 euros.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com) Corrections & Amplifications

This article was corrected at 08:03 a.m. ET to fix the spelling of the company name.

0916 GMT - Delivery Hero's cut to its 2025 adjusted Ebitda guidance raises questions, UBS analysts write in a note. The German food-delivery company now expects adjusted Ebitda to be in the range of 900 million euros and 940 million euros, down from 975 million euros to 1.025 billion euros, citing foreign exchange rate headwinds. This exchange rate pressure was acknowledged earlier in the year, so the reason driving the downgrade is unclear, the analysts add. The group's solid first-half results add to this uncertainty, they say. Shares are down 0.8% at 23.25 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0039 GMT - Summerset's annuity earnings in 1H of FY 2025 were its weakest in five years, but Forsyth Barr says the retirement-village operator's result wasn't all bad news. "Summerset stuck its neck out and guided for largely flat net debt from FY 2025 onwards," analyst Aaron Ibbotson says. That is a marked change from the past five years, when net debt increased by a factor of 3X. Summerset's net tangible assets continued to grow strongly, in contrast to rival Ryman Healthcare. Contracted sales in July and August are up 30% on year. "If Summerset delivers on expectations of a substantial acceleration in cash generation and sales over the next 12 months, not least in Australia, the stock could do well," Forsyth Barr says. "But it is a big if." Summerset is up 2.7% at NZ$10.94. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ) Corrections & Amplifications

This was corrected at 0047 GMT. The original version misstated the reporting period as FY 2025 intead of 1H.

2237 GMT [Dow Jones]--Private hospital operator Ramsay Health Care expects earnings in Australia to rise in FY 2026. Jefferies thinks any improvement will be modest due to labor cost pressures. "Ramsay seen significant wage rises from all public payers, which likely has flow-on effects for the private sector," analyst David Stanton says. Wage bargaining negotiations have been finalized in New South Wales and Western Australia states, which represent 27% of group Ebit. Deals remain outstanding in Queensland and Victoria, also representing 27% of Ebit. Also, a new contract for Joondalup, Western Australia, will deal a A$37 million hit to Ebit. Jefferies retains a hold call on Ramsay, cuts its price target by 11% to A$34.50/share. Ramsay ended Thursday at A$34.09. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1821 GMT - Cooper Cos.'s solid margins and improving free cash flow should help the company's stock despite the company's poor third-quarter earnings, say Stifel analysts, who label the stock as a "buy." The company's adjusted operating margin of 26.1% beat Wall Street estimates and Cooper said operating efficiencies are expected to continue through a restructuring in 2026. Free cash flow also beat expectations by a wide margin with working-capital efficiencies expected to continue bolstering the company's cash in coming years. The stock is down 11%, to $66.27, Thursday. That decline gives investors an opportunity to buy, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0817 GMT - Reports of yet another tax rise to shore up the U.K.'s finances are weighing on financial stocks on Friday, putting pressure on U.K. banking shares. "The drip feed of potential tax rises to be included in this budget have dominated the papers this month, however, so far, the FTSE 100 has been resilient to this," XTB analyst Kathleen Brooks writes. Yet, it now seems the Treasury Chief is going after the index's big hitters like banks, she says. This could weigh on the index ahead of the autumn budget, the analyst says. The FTSE 100 slips 0.3% in morning trade, pulled down by a 4.7% fall for NatWest, while Lloyds slips 3.8% and Barclays is down 3.1%. International lenders HSBC and Standard Chartered both slip around 1%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0735 GMT - U.K. banking stocks trade lower shortly after market open on Friday. "Overnight weakness in sterling would normally support the FTSE 100 given its proliferation of overseas earnings, but reports of a potential windfall tax on the banks more than offset any hopes of a bright open," Interactive Investor analyst Richard Hunter writes in a market comment. Lloyds Banking Group, NatWest and Barclays sank to the bottom of London's blue-chip FTSE 100 index and lost 3.4%, 4.0% and 2.8%, respectively. "Unfortunately, any such rumors are likely to have an exaggerated impact given the government's obvious need to raise more income in an attempt to mitigate its financial difficulties," Hunter adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0601 GMT - AXA's recent share-price fall is overdone and offers an opportunity, Berenberg says in a research note after concerns about France's budgetary constraints sank the stocks of companies exposed to the country's government bonds. The insurer's balance-sheet exposure to French government bonds is just 13% of its overall government bond exposure, which is relatively limited, analyst Michael Huttner writes. AXA also has very limited sensitivity to Eurozone sovereign spreads and limited exposure to a potential French corporate-tax increase because only a quarter of its earnings--and an even lower proportion of its taxable income--relate to its operations in its home country. "With its strong solvency and broad geographic diversification, it is well-placed to face any challenges," he notes. Shares are down 6.3% in the week to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0520 GMT - Swedbank is making efforts to expand into a younger, higher-margin customer base in the face of mortgage market-share pressures, Citi says in a note after the Swedish lender agreed to buy Barclays's stake in their Nordic consumer-credit joint venture. "We think this acquisition may to some extent offset the negative timing effects we foresee playing out towards year-end, shifting investor focus," analysts write. While the impact on profitability is small, the consolidation of Entercard is set to lift consensus estimates for net interest income and fees. The cost impact is likely to be largely absorbed by future recoveries in value-added tax, they say. This deal, which comes after its acquisition of digital mortgage credit institution Stabelo, aligns with its pledge to capture a greater share of consumer value, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0245 GMT - RHB Bank's 2H net profit is projected to rise 6.1% from the previous half to 1.65 billion ringgit, CGS International analyst Winson Ng says in a note. Still, that would be 1.1% lower on year due to the Malaysian central bank's policy rate cut in July, the analyst says. RHB expects 2H key earnings catalysts to be stronger investment income and higher fees from insurance and Takaful collaboration, he notes. Despite July's rate cut, the bank is maintaining its 2025 ROE target of 10.4%-10.8%. To meet this, RHB would need to post 2.0 billion ringgit-2.15 billion ringgit net profit in 2H, he adds. CGS maintains an add rating on RHB Bank and keeps its target price at MYR7.36. Shares are 0.2% lower at MYR6.51. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1649 GMT - Industry brand loyalty rates declined slightly in the first half of 2025 following last year's rebound, according to a S&P Global Mobility analysis of new vehicle registration data June. The industry's brand loyalty rate through June stood at 51.1%, down 1.4 percentage points from the same period in 2024. The decrease marks a reversal from 2024's year-over-year gain, showing continued volatility in loyalty trends as the market continues to stabilize post-pandemic. Both mainstream and luxury brands contributed to the decline. The number of households returning to market rose 4.2% year-over-year, representing the third consecutive year of improvement. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1405 GMT - Falling mortgage rates have pushed the median U.S. monthly mortgage payment down to $2,616, its lowest level since the start of the year, according to Redfin. The weekly average mortgage rate is sitting at 6.58%, its lowest level in 10 months. Lower housing costs are bringing some house hunters off the sidelines. Pending home sales rose 1.6% year over year during the four weeks ending Wednesday. That marks two straight months of increases in pending sales after they fell for most of 2025. But many buyers are still waiting on the sidelines. A lot of prospective buyers are waiting to see if the Fed cuts interest rates in September to buy. But Redfin economists say today's mortgage rates likely already reflect the upcoming rate cut. On the selling side, new listings are up 1.9% year over year. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1140 GMT - Switzerland's midcap insurers first-half results aren't set to be particularly exciting, though the subsector could still benefit from strong currency trends, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a note. Helvetia and Baloise--which recently agreed to merge--and Swiss Life will publish their results in September. Swiss primary insurers have had good performances since the start of the year, and their shares have outperformed the Stoxx 600 insurance sub-index, analyst Michele Ballatore writes. Swiss Life's results could see some weakness based on a strong comparative, while the focus for Helvetia and Baloise will be on their upcoming integration, he adds. "Operationally, it should be a relatively benign quarter in [property and casualty] with some headwinds from FX," Ballatore says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0900 GMT - Santander's return on tangible equity could reach 20%, Morgan Stanley says in a research note. The Spanish bank could unveil a more than 18% ROTE target at its February investor day, analyst Alvaro Serrano writes. Thanks to the integration of TSB in the U.K., the deployment of its Gravity digital banking platform, and the normalization of its Brazil earnings as the U.S. Federal Reserve has opened the door to lower interest rates, the analyst sees a path to a ROTE of 18% to 19%. This could increase by a further percentage point if the profitability of the bank's U.S. retail operations is addressed, he adds. Shares have risen 84% since the start of the year. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0849 GMT - PPF's decision to sell its stake in German broadcaster ProSiebenSat.1 Media supports consolidation efforts, Equita analyst Milo Silvestre writes in a note. The Czech investment conglomerate is tendering around 15.7% of ProSiebenSat.1's share capital into Netherlands-based media broadcasting group MFE-MediaForEurope's $2.4 billion takeover offer. PPF's participation in the takeover bid is supportive for MFE's stock, giving MFE control of ProSiebenSat.1 and removing uncertainty over the asset's future, he says. The consolidation is also strategically sound, strengthening MFE's presence in one of Europe's largest free-to-air advertising markets, he adds. MFE and ProSiebenSat.1 shares rise 6.4% and 2.1%, respectively. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0837 GMT - Bitcoin exchange-traded funds are seeing improved demand after recent capital outflows, Deus X Pay director James Madden says. Spot bitcoin ETFs from Fidelity and BlackRock have broken their outflow streak, bringing in $219 million in daily inflows, he says. "This shift reflects a renewed interest in bitcoin, demonstrating that these funds are gaining traction after a challenging period," Madden says. The influx of capital into these ETFs signals confidence in bitcoin's long-term potential and reinforces the narrative of institutional adoption of cryptocurrencies, he says. Bitcoin rises 0.7% to $113,225, recovering after hitting a nearly seven-week low of $108,829 Tuesday, LSEG data show. However, it remains well below the record high of $124,480 reached on August 14.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0216 GMT - Tenaga Nasional is set to gain from Malaysia's energy transition as the country upgrades power grids to support the government's renewable energy targets, TA Securities analyst Hafriz Hezry says in a note. Rapid growth in data centers is also expected to drive demand for new generation capacity, with Tenaga's power generation unit Genco seen as a frontrunner in coming power plant projects, he says. Meanwhile, Tenaga has reapplied for tax incentives following a court ruling on its outstanding tax issue. In the worst case, rejection could result in potential exposure of up to 10 billion ringgit, he notes. TA Securities maintains a buy rating on Tenaga Nasional with an unchanged target price of MYR17.30. Shares are 0.3% lower at MYR13.40.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1923 GMT - Oil futures settle higher after spending much of the session in the red, with the market watching increased strikes in the Russia-Ukraine war just weeks after hopes were raised for peace talks, while prospects for looser oil balances after the high-demand summer season keep buyers cautious. "Traders are glued to that narrow range," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. "It's the classic push and pull-bulls and bears in a stalemate while everyone waits for the next shoe to drop." WTI settles up 0.7% at $64.60 a barrel. Brent rises 0.8% to $67.62 ahead of tomorrow's October contract expiration. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1244 GMT - Oil futures are slightly lower with the market focused on Russia-Ukraine and expectations for looser market balances after the summer. Russia-related tariffs and sanctions, along with attacks on Russian oil facilities are keeping crude prices resilient "despite the looming oversupply that should lower prices, particularly after the Mideast summer burn for cooling purposes and the end of the summer driving season," Anthony Yuen of Citi Research says in a note. He attributes that to OPEC+ output increases being less than expected, while "the amount of Indian diversification away from Russian oil purchases could also be greater than reported, as reflected in a much tighter Brent-Dubai spread." Citi keeps its average 3Q price forecast for Brent at $66 a barrel, and sees the benchmark averaging $63 in 4Q as "oversupply should start to exert greater impact on the oil supply-demand balance into year end." Brent is off 0.3% at $67.87 and WTI is off 0.2% at $64.04.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1035 GMT - Drax's agreement with the U.K. government to keep its North Yorkshire power plant open after 2027 through the use of subsidies could face additional political scrutiny, Jefferies analysts write. This is after the Financial Conduct Authority launched an investigation into certain historical statements regarding its biomass sourcing and the compliance of some annual reports. The investigation appears to be at a very early stage, which means limited information is currently available, the analysts write. Shares trade down 9% at 630.50 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0939 GMT - Orsted's shares will remain under pressure over the near term as it battles uncertainty in the U.S. over its Revolution Wind project and undertakes a rights issue, Berenberg analysts write. However, under all scenarios, including the unlikely situation that it can't divest assets and is forced to cancel its U.S. offshore projects, the Danish energy company will be able to cover capital expenditure through 2027, they write. This is because of its planned $9.4 billion rights issue, they add. A share price of 172 Danish krone is seen as a floor in its valuation, they write. The analysts upgrade the stock's rating to buy, from hold, but with a reduced target price of 225 krone, from 250 krone. Shares trade up 1.9% at 192.65 krone. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0848 GMT - Shares in power producer Drax trade lower after the U.K.'s financial watchdog started an investigation into the company's biomass sourcing and recent annual reports. The probe could increase uncertainty over an agreement with the U.K. government to extend the company's power station, RBC Capital Markets analysts say. There is no indication on why it was launched or when it will conclude, the analysts say. Shares trade down 9% at 640 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0350 GMT - Mineral Resources needs higher commodity prices to make material inroads in reducing its debt load, Citi analyst Kate McCutcheon says in a note. She forecasts fairly flat net debt for the iron ore and lithium producer using Citi's base-case forecasts. However, using spot prices, Mineral Resources would reach its net debt target by FY 2027-end, she says. The miner "says there's no present need to raise equity and is reviewing opportunities for inorganic de-leveraging," says McCutcheon. "Our base case remains that the 2027 bonds are refinanced." Citi has a sell-high risk rating on Mineral Resources, with a A$34.00 target. The stock is up 0.4% at A$37.02. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0340 GMT - Lynas Rare Earths' equity raise makes sense given the recent improvement in market sentiment, Citi analyst Samuel Schubert says. However, the timing and structure of the raise suggests Lynas may not benefit from the same favorable terms as U.S. peer MP Materials, namely a US$110/kilogram neodymium-praseodymium price floor, says Schubert. He highlights the lack of direct government participation or official rare-earth price floor backing the A$750 million capital raise. "The market may perceive this as a signal that government support is not forthcoming immediately, reinforcing the view that sentiment has peaked and that near-term valuation is vulnerable to reset," Schubert says. Citi reiterates a sell rating on Lynas, although it raises its target sharply to A$9.50/share from A$5.50 before. Lynas is down 6.1% at A$13.83. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0302 GMT - Gold Road's 1H accounts include "little misses everywhere," say Moelis Australia analysts Paul Hissey and Nic McRostie. "But does it matter?" they say. The result is unlikely to get much investor interest given Gold Road has agreed to be acquired by South Africa's Gold Fields. Without that offer, "we might be more inclined to call this a 'soft' result," say the analysts. However, the only investors still holding Gold Road are likely to be those who are keen to see the Gold Fields deal through to the end, they say. Moelis Australia has a hold rating and a A$3.20 target on Gold Road. The stock is down 0.2% at A$3.325. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1238 GMT - Sibanye Stillwater delivered a relatively strong set of first-half results with Ebitda and free cash flow beating consensus expectations, RBC Capital Markets analysts write. A positive element was management appearing to open the door to delaying the commissioning and production at its Keliber plant given the current lithium market conditions, they say. This suggests that the South African precious-metals miner is willing to restructure low-margin operations, they say. The company's exposure to gold and platinum group metals, combined with the potential for a portfolio restructure under its new management, should drive a re-rating of its shares. Shares trade down 2.2% at 3,402 rand ($192). (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0900 GMT - Hochschild Mining is unlikely to get full operations up and running at its Mara Rosa mine in Brazil until the second half 2026, Berenberg analysts write. Despite previously flagging woes at the open-pit gold mine, Wednesday's production guidance cut and raised cost expectations were more severe than expected and missed consensus expectations, they add. Investors will likely welcome an updated long-term plan for the mine in 2026 once the operational outlook is clearer, the analysts write. Operational challenges aren't the only headwind the miner faces, with inflation in Argentina pushing costs higher, they write. Shares trade down 2.2% at 271.80 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0609 GMT - Schaeffler AG shares look attractive going into the company's capital markets day mid-September, Citi analysts say in a research note. The German manufacturer of rolling element bearings is set to present midterm targets and provide updates on defense exposure and on its in-house humanoid robot startup, Citi says. The risk-reward balance in the shares seems attractive going into the event, the analysts say. Citi raises its recommendation on the stock to buy from neutral and increases the target price to 6.75 euros from 5.00 euros. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0238 GMT - Bumi Armada's stock lacks catalysts, Maybank IB analyst Jeremie Yap writes in a report. The offshore energy facilities and services provider maintains a sizable fleet of floating storage and offloading. However, Bumi Armada's ability to secure new projects could be constrained due to operational hiccups and its narrower global presence, Yap says. The company could have limited opportunities in major FPSO tenders, potentially leading to a lack of positive share-price catalysts over the next 12 to 24 months and a declining order book. Maybank IB downgrade the stock to hold from buy and lowers its target price to MYR0.39 from MYR0.73. Shares are flat at MYR0.37.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0131 GMT - Capital A is poised for stronger 2H earnings, driven by seasonally robust travel demand, a weaker dollar, and softer jet fuel prices, Hong Leong Investment Bank analyst Daniel Wong says in a note. Capital A awaits Thai SEC approval for the planned sale of its aviation segment to AirAsia X, with management optimistic about the completion in 4Q, he notes. Following the sale, Capital A plans to apply for exiting its financial distress status by end-2025, which could lift investor interest, he says. Its brand management unit is also exploring new licensing and franchising opportunities with third parties, while management is reviving a plan to list the unit on Nasdaq within the next six to eight months, he adds. Hong Leong maintains a buy rating on Capital A and keeps its target price at MYR1.68. Shares are 1.8% higher at MYR0.85.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0029 GMT - Air New Zealand's challenges are likely to extend through December, but Forsyth Barr finds reasons to be optimistic about the medium-term outlook. Analyst Andy Bowley says Air New Zealand will navigate the worst of its engine maintenance crisis in 1H FY 2026. Beyond then, however, things should brighten. The engine issue will gradually improve and be fully resolved by 2H FY 2028, Forsyth Barr says. Air New Zealand will reap more of its transformation benefits. Domestic demand should begin a cyclical recovery, and Air New Zealand's so-called "premiumization strategy" should bring incremental opportunities to lift revenue, the bank says. Still, Forsyth Barr stays at neutral, noting Air New Zealand's return on capital will stay suppressed even as earnings improve over the next 3-4 years. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2248 GMT - Jefferies is a big fan of everything about car dealership Eargers Automotive--except its valuation. Eagers's share price rose 12% on Thursday after beating expectations in 1H. Revenue of A$6.5 billion was 7% ahead of consensus hopes, while gross profit was 2% ahead of estimates. Jefferies says Eagers controls 14% of new vehicle sales in Australia, compared to the largest U.S. dealer, which has 2% of the market there. It's also big in used-vehicle sales. That makes it attractive for auto makers looking to establish a retail presence in Australia. Still, Jefferies says Eagers's valuation metrics following Thursday's short squeeze just look too high. "The market appears to be pricing in deals yet to happen," says analyst John Campbell. "We can't get there." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1649 GMT - Industry brand loyalty rates declined slightly in the first half of 2025 following last year's rebound, according to a S&P Global Mobility analysis of new vehicle registration data June. The industry's brand loyalty rate through June stood at 51.1%, down 1.4 percentage points from the same period in 2024. The decrease marks a reversal from 2024's year-over-year gain, showing continued volatility in loyalty trends as the market continues to stabilize post-pandemic. Both mainstream and luxury brands contributed to the decline. The number of households returning to market rose 4.2% year-over-year, representing the third consecutive year of improvement. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

data.title: Foxconn Industrial Internet's Market Capitalization Hits Trillion Yuan
data.description:
By Sherry Qin

Foxconn Industrial Internet, the manufacturing arm of Foxconn Technology Group, became the latest company to reach a market capitalization of 1 trillion yuan in China amid an AI frenzy.

Shares of FII surged 10% on Friday, the daily maximum permitted in Shanghai, to close at 53.83 yuan, taking its market capitalization to 1.07 trillion yuan, equivalent to $150.06 billion. Its shares have risen over 150% so far this quarter.

FII became the first tech-hardware company to join the trillion-yuan club and amid China's push to develop artificial intelligence and rising risk appetite, which sent the Shanghai Composite Index to a 10-year high earlier this month.

A total of 13 Shanghai- and Shenzhen-listed companies have a market cap of over 1 trillion yuan, according to financial-data platform Wind Information. State-owned banks and insurance companies make up more than half of the trillion-yuan club, with Industrial and Commercial Bank of China leading with a market cap of nearly 2.5 trillion yuan.

Other companies that have market caps above that benchmark include liquor maker Kweichow Moutai, Chinese battery giant CATL and electric-vehicle maker BYD.

FII became the 10th-most valuable company listed in mainland China as of market close on Friday, according to Wind.

Foxconn, FII's Taiwan-based parent company, formally known as Hon Hai Precision Industry, is the world's largest contract electronics maker and best known for assembling Apple's iPhones.

Foxconn has diversified into high-growth areas such as AI and now plays an increasingly important role in building AI servers for U.S. tech giants including Amazon and Nvidia. Cloud and networking products, which includes AI servers, became Foxconn's top revenue contributor for the first time in the second quarter, overtaking the segment that includes smartphones.

After Foxconn said its Nvidia GB200 rack shipments are expected to improve substantially in the third quarter, analysts think FII could be one of the few players staying ahead of the curve in improving production yields, even as its peers have offered a subdued outlook.

Improvement in yields could boost FII's market share and lead to stronger profitability in the second half of the year and beyond, Citi analysts said.

Despite the sharp rally recently, Citi thinks FII's shares are still cheap, and raised its target price to 60.00 yuan from 48.00 yuan.

China's red-hot AI sector and ambition to make advanced chips at home have fanned investor enthusiasm for related stocks.

Alongside FII, shares of AI chip designer Cambricon Technologies have more than doubled over the past month to overtake Kweichow Moutai as the most valuable stock in China.

Analysts think China's stock rally still has further to run as more liquidity could flow into the equity market due to retail investors relocating assets into stocks from the property market and deposit accounts.

HSBC said AI will remain a key investment theme in the Chinese market, especially in AI infrastructure given increased capital expenditure by cloud-service providers and rising AI penetration.

Write to Sherry Qin at sherry.qin@wsj.com

data.title: Hang Seng Tech Index Ends 0.5% Higher at 5674.31
Spain Current Account Surplus Narrows in June
data.description:
Spain’s current account surplus narrowed to €5.03 billion in June 2025 from €5.44 billion in the same month of the previous year.

The goods and services surplus fell to €6.37 billion from €7.29 billion in June 2024.

On the other hand, the deficit in primary and secondary income declined to €1.35 billion from €1.85 billion.

data.title: SNG: South Africa: Sibanye Stillwater cuts losses to $211mln
data.description:
Staff Writer

Sibanye Stillwater said it narrowed its half-year loss to $211m, mainly due to production credits extended to its US palladium business as well as the restructuring of its South African mines.

The diversified miner's loss in the six months to June 30 narrowed from $372m the year before, when it booked a $407m impairment on its US operations after cutting its forecast for palladium prices.

Under the Inflation Reduction Act enacted in 2022, the US offers credits as an incentive for the domestic production of critical minerals, including palladium.

"The positive financial outcomes from solid operational management and decisive restructuring were amplified by the incorporation of Section 45X credits in terms of the Inflation Reduction Act," Sibanye said in a statement.

Sibanye said a total $285m in combined estimated credits from the 2023 financial year had been recognised, boosting profitability for the first half of 2025. Cash payments are expected in 2026, it added.

All rights reserved. © 2022. Bizcommunity.com Provided by SyndiGate Media Inc. (Syndigate.info).

data.title: Dow Jones Top Energy Headlines at 4 AM ET: Nvidia and Google Back Nuclear Fusion Start-Up | Oil ...
data.description:
Nvidia and Google Back Nuclear Fusion Start-Up

Commonwealth Fusion Systems said it isn't eyeing a near-term initial public offering

----

Oil Futures Stage Late Turnaround

Oil futures rose after a day of losses, with investors focused both on increased strikes in the Russia-Ukraine war and the prospect of a global supply glut.

----

Oil Glut Concerns Cloud Price Outlook Despite Geopolitical Risks

A survey compiled by WSJ showed Brent crude is expected to average $63.57 a barrel in the fourth quarter, while West Texas Intermediate is seen at $60.30 a barrel. That is down from last month's projections of $64.13 and $61.11, respectively.

----

U.S. Natural Gas Rallies on Small Inventory Build

U.S. natural gas futures rose as a weekly storage build came in below expectations, reducing the inventory surplus over the five-year average for a second consecutive week.

----

Another Old Nuclear Reactor Is Coming Back. These Stocks Could Benefit.

The electricity needed for the tech sector's artificial intelligence race is turning reactors that were once unprofitable albatrosses into potential profit-drivers.

----

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on oil futures, Cnooc, QL Resources and more in the latest Market Talks covering energy and utilities.

----

U.S. Nuclear Energy Drive to Spur Uranium Enrichment

The growing need for electricity to power artificial intelligence and massive data centers in the U.S. promises to usher nuclear energy into a new era of expansion, opening up opportunities for the industries that provide the fuel for the plants.

----

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Fall for Second Straight Week

U.S. crude oil inventories fell by 2.4 million barrels, more than the estimated drop of 1.9 million barrels.

----

Exxon Held Secret Talks With Rosneft About Going Back to Russia

Resuming business in Russia would mark a dramatic rapprochement after Exxon's messy breakup with Moscow when Putin attacked Ukraine in 2022.

----

Puerto Rico Utility Bondholders Break Off Deal After Oversight Board Purge

BlackRock, Nuveen and other creditors ditched a previous deal to write down over $8 billion in electric revenue bonds.

----

U.S. and South Korean Nuclear Companies Sign Agreement

U.S. and South Korean officials have pledged cooperation in several industries, which could include nuclear energy and shipbuilding.

----

The Ukrainian Attacks That Are Forcing Russia to Ration Its Fuel

The late Sen. John McCain once called Russia "a gas station with nukes." Now, because of Ukrainian attacks, it must ration fuel.

data.title: JPM says Data Center exposure still key to mid-term utilities growth, picks Legrand
data.description:
** J.P.Morgan assumes 'Neutral' coverage on electrical equipment suppliers ABB and Schneider Electric and rates Legrand 'Overweight', as data center exposure should drive margin growth

** JPM sees DC (Data Center) growth continuing to be strongly accretive to the growth outlook of the electrical sector for several years, most likely in the 20-40% range

** It estimates France's Legrand and Schneider to have the highest exposure to DC at about 24% of revenue, with peers ABB and Siemens at about 6% and 2% respectively

** As typically high-margin segments begin to recover—alongside continued growth in data centers— the broker sees upward revisions to Legrand’s earnings forecasts

** While Schneider's growth remains attractive, outperforming peers in recent years thanks to a superior portofolio and exposure to the fastest growing end markets, JPM expects peers organic growth to catch up in 2026

** JPM notes the absence of a planned CMD for Schneider which would allow to address questions around the co's leverage and cost profile, while Siemen's planned update 'will include key portfolio changes which will drive the investment case and forward multiples'

data.title: China's military, coast guard patrol disputed South China Sea atoll
data.description:
China's military and coast guard said they conducted patrols on Friday in the waters around the disputed Scarborough Shoal in the busy waterway of the South China Sea.

The coast guard has stepped up law enforcement patrols in the area since the beginning of August, it said in a statement, vowing to "safeguard China's territorial sovereignty and maritime rights and interests".

China sent its naval and air forces on "combat readiness" patrols in the waters and airspace around the atoll, the Southern Theatre Command said in a separate statement.

The Philippine embassy in Beijing did not immediately respond to emailed queries about the statements.

A Chinese coast guard vessel was damaged this month in a collision with a Chinese naval vessel near the Scarborough Shoal, in the first known crash between China's ships there.

China claims almost the entire South China Sea, despite overlapping claims by Brunei, Malaysia, the Philippines and Vietnam.
